reasoning:
For the 30-day period ending June 29 (which largely covers May 30 - June 28), the evidence heavily points to a positive resolution. In early June 2025, Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin (JNIM) intensified a historic regional offensive, launching massive attacks on Malian Armed Forces (FAMa). One coordinated series of attacks on June 1-2 alone reportedly killed over 60 (and claimed by JNIM as >100) soldiers across multiple bases, with ACLED and ISW designating the recent weeks as 'one of the deadliest-ever 30-day periods' in the Sahel. Coupled with high state-led retaliation typical in these scenarios, fatalities will almost certainly exceed the 360-day average of ~331.

For July 22, the 30-day window lies entirely in the future, but the ongoing momentum of the JNIM offensive and subsequent Wagner/FAMa sweeping operations suggests an elevated likelihood of continued high violence, keeping the expectation above the mean.

For periods further out (September and December), uncertainty increases, causing the estimate to regress to the statistical base rate. In typical right-skewed fatality distributions, the median is below the mean (as the mean is raised by extreme outlier events). Consequently, a randomly selected 30-day period has a base rate somewhat below 50% (around 40-45%) of exceeding the annual mean. Probabilities steadily reflect this regression toward the base rate over time.
